planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic medium in partial to full shade in zone 10–12, or grow as an annual indoors or on a shaded patio in cooler zones; Parrot Impatiens (African Impatiens, Congo Impatiens) is a large, spectacular, shade-loving tender perennial from the rainforests of tropical Central Africa — bearing elongated, dramatic flowers with brilliant red pouched spurs tipped with bright yellow, strongly resembling tiny, hovering parrots — continuously blooming in warm conditions.

directions: Root Impatiens niamniamensis tip cuttings in moist, well-drained, humus-rich potting mix in a warm, shaded location; keep evenly moist — never waterlogged — and protect from any frost; pinch growing tips regularly to encourage bushy growth; fertilize monthly with a balanced liquid fertilizer during active growth. Parrot Impatiens is one of the most extraordinary of all shade-loving plants — the unusual, elongated, hooded flowers with their brilliant scarlet-and-yellow coloring genuinely resemble small parrots or tiny shoes, creating a remarkable conversation piece in the shaded garden or container. Native to the dense rainforests of Uganda, DRC, and adjacent Central Africa, where it grows in deep, humid shade in rich, moist forest floor soil, it is perfectly adapted to the dappled shade of tropical and subtropical gardens. The species is a reliable hummingbird plant in its adopted range; in Florida and South Texas, it attracts ruby-throated and rufous hummingbirds. Unlike many Impatiens, it has robust, somewhat succulent stems and grows into a substantial plant 2–3 feet tall; it does not wilt as readily in partial sun as the more tender I. walleriana but still performs best in partial to full shade with consistent moisture. In zone 10–12, it grows as a perennial shrub; in cooler areas, an excellent container plant for shaded patios and sunrooms.

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, freely draining, acidic to neutral soil or container mix in zone 8–11; Sutherland Begonia is a compact, trailing, tuberous begonia native to the moist forests, cliff faces, and stream banks of the Drakensberg mountains of KwaZulu-Natal and the Eastern Cape of South Africa — bearing abundant, small, bright-orange to salmon-orange flowers on reddish stems throughout summer and fall in partial shade.

directions: Plant Begonia sutherlandii tubers in partial shade in humus-rich, freely draining, slightly acidic potting mix or garden soil; keep evenly moist during active growth; allow to dry down after fall frost or when foliage yellows, then store tubers dry and cool for winter in zone 8–9; repot in spring. Sutherland Begonia is one of the more cold-tolerant of all tuberous begonias — its native Drakensberg habitat experiences occasional light frost — and performs well in zone 8 with good drainage and a protective mulch over the dormant tubers. The bright, clear-orange single flowers are produced in cheerful clusters throughout summer and into fall, even in quite deep shade where few other flowering plants perform; this makes it exceptionally valuable for the shaded garden. The trailing, red-stemmed habit makes it ideal for hanging baskets, container edges, rock walls, and woodland garden borders. Unlike the large-flowered hybrid tuberous begonias which require careful culture, Sutherland Begonia is relatively easy to grow and increases by offset tubers over time. In fall, distinctive bulbils (tiny aerial tubers) form in the leaf axils and can be collected and potted to produce new plants. The species has been used extensively in hybridization programs to introduce trailing habit and orange-flame coloring into modern begonia hybrids.
sources: South African National Biodiversity Institute (SANBI) — Begonia sutherlandii

subcategory: African iconic ancient dryland multi-use tree

planting.note: Sow seeds (scarified with sandpaper or soaked 24 hours in warm water) in spring in full sun in deep, very well-drained, sandy to loamy, dry to moderately moist soil in zone 10–12; Baobab is the most iconic tree of sub-Saharan Africa — a massive, long-lived deciduous tree with a distinctive swollen, water-storing trunk reaching immense girth — producing large, hard-shelled fruits containing a dry, white, powder-coated pulp extremely rich in Vitamin C and calcium, used as a food and commercial superfood ingredient across Africa.

directions: Plant Baobab in deep, freely draining, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; they are highly drought-tolerant once established and dislike any root waterlogging — excellent drainage is non-negotiable; water moderately during summer active growth and reduce or stop watering in winter when deciduous; container-grown Baobabs make striking bonsai or patio specimens for decades before size becomes an issue. Baobab is one of the most extraordinary trees on Earth — capable of living 2,000–5,000+ years (and possibly far longer), storing thousands of liters of water in its massive, spongy, swollen trunk, and providing food, medicine, fiber, and shelter for both people and wildlife across its native sub-Saharan Africa range. The large, white, bat-pollinated flowers open at night and are strongly fragrant; they are followed by the football-sized, hard, hanging fruits — known commercially as 'baobab fruit' or 'monkey bread' — containing dry, chalky-white pulp naturally rich in Vitamin C (6x more than oranges per weight), calcium, potassium, and antioxidants. The pulp, leaves, seeds, bark, and roots all have documented food and medicinal uses across Africa; commercial baobab fruit powder has grown into a global superfood industry. In zone 10–12, it makes a spectacular, extraordinarily long-lived specimen tree; young plants grow slowly but rewards patient gardeners with one of the planet's most remarkable organisms.
sources: FAO — Baobab: Adansonia digitata, a multi-purpose food tree
Baobab (Adansonia digitata L.) — Kew Science monograph
African Forest Forum — Adansonia digitata sustainable uses

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 10–12; Soncoya (Cabeza de Negro, Sincuya) is a large tropical fruit tree native to the dry Pacific coast forests of Mexico, Guatemala, El Salvador, and Costa Rica — bearing very large, spiny, round to oval fruits with a distinctively sweet, dense, orange-yellow custard flesh and a strong, complex aroma — an underappreciated relative of the more widely grown cherimoya and sugar apple.

directions: Plant Annona purpurea in full sun in fertile, moist but well-drained soil; water regularly during establishment and during fruiting; the tree is more drought-tolerant than Cherimoya or Sugar Apple once established; hand-pollinate flowers with a small brush during the female phase (when petals are slightly opening) to ensure fruit set, as natural pollinators may be absent outside the native range. Soncoya is the largest of the commercially relevant Annona fruits — with individual fruits commonly weighing 2–5 kg and occasionally much more — distinguished from the better-known custard apples by its dark-green, spiny surface (resembling a chestnut or sea urchin) and very dense, deep-orange to apricot-yellow flesh. The aroma is powerful and characteristic: sweet, musky, and fruity, sometimes described as a combination of mango, pineapple, and vanilla custard. The flesh is eaten fresh with a spoon scooped from the halved fruit; the flavor is very sweet and rich, though the large brown seeds must be removed. Soncoya is native to tropical dry forests of the Pacific coast of Central America and Mexico, making it better adapted to a pronounced dry season than most other Annonas; it performs well in zones with a dry winter season (zone 10–12 in Florida, Hawaii, and South Texas). Rarely seen in cultivation outside its native range but occasionally available from rare fruit nurseries.
sources: Morton, Julia F., 'Fruits of Warm Climates' (Creative Resource Systems, 1987)
Rare Fruit Council International — Annona purpurea
CATIE (Costa Rica) — tropical fruit species database — Annona purpurea

subcategory: Mexican dye cactus edible nopal pad

planting.note: Root dried pad cuttings in dry, sandy, very well-drained soil in full sun in zone 9–12; Cochineal Nopal (Tuna Mansa, Nopal Cardón) is a tall, tree-like, spineless or nearly-spineless cactus from Mexico — the primary host plant historically cultivated for the cochineal insect (Dactylopius coccus) that produces the brilliant red carmine dye used in textiles, cosmetics, and food coloring — with edible, mild pads (nopales) and small red fruits.

directions: Root a dried Nopalea cochenillifera pad cutting (allow cut end to callus for 1–2 weeks before planting) in dry, coarse sandy soil in full sun; plant only 2–3 inches deep; water sparingly until established, then infrequently; the plant is highly drought-tolerant once established and susceptible to root rot in wet soils. Cochineal Nopal is the cactus at the center of one of the most remarkable stories in natural dye history — the basis of carmine (E120), the brilliant scarlet-red dye that became one of the most valuable trade commodities between the Americas and Europe after Spanish conquest, worth more than silver by weight in 16th-century Spain. The cactus is the host of the cochineal insect (Dactylopius coccus), tiny scale insects that feed on the cactus pads and produce carminic acid as a defense compound — the source of intense, colorfast red dye. Pre-Columbian Aztec and Mixtec civilizations had cultivated cochineal production for centuries before European contact; after conquest, the Spanish maintained it as a closely guarded trade secret for two centuries. Carmine remains important today in natural food coloring, cosmetics (lipstick, rouge), and textiles; it is the red in many 'natural' food colorings. The pads (nopales) are edible like other Opuntia species and can be harvested as young, tender pads for cooking; small red fruits also edible. A large, dramatic, sculptural cactus for hot, dry gardens.
sources: Amy Butler Greenfield, 'A Perfect Red: Empire, Espionage and the Quest for the Color Scarlet' (HarperCollins, 2005)

planting.note: Root cuttings in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in zone 6–10; Hart's Pennyroyal is an unusual Mediterranean aquatic mint — native to stream banks, wet meadows, and seasonal wetlands from Iberia and southern France through North Africa — bearing very narrow, rush-like leaves and small spikes of pale lavender flowers, with a strong, clean spearmint fragrance and excellent culinary and ornamental value in moist to wet garden situations.

directions: Plant Mentha cervina in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; it tolerates standing water and grows well at the margins of ponds, in bog gardens, or in any reliably moist position; contain in pots as with other mints to prevent spreading; harvest stems for fresh use throughout the growing season. Hart's Pennyroyal is a distinctive and underused culinary mint — rare in cultivation but increasingly recognized for its ornamental value and culinary merit. Unlike most mints, it is adapted to aquatic or near-aquatic conditions and grows naturally in the beds of seasonal streams that dry in summer and flood in winter across the Iberian Peninsula, southern France, and North Africa. The very narrow, almost rush-like leaves are unlike any other mint and give the plant a unique, fine texture in the water garden. Culinary use closely mirrors that of spearmint: the leaves have a clean, fresh, strong spearmint fragrance with overtones of peppermint, and can be used fresh in teas, cocktails (particularly in Iberian cuisine), desserts, and savory dishes. In Portugal and Spain, it is used to make 'hortelã-da-ribeira' tea (riverside mint tea). Unlike some Mentha species, it does not spread as aggressively as peppermint or spearmint; still, container culture is advisable in mixed herb gardens. A charming, refined, and genuinely useful mint for the aquatic herb garden.
sources: Plants of the World Online — Mentha cervina
